speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor's name is George Steinberg. The interviewer is Donna Kenny, and we are doing this interview in Longmeadow, Mass. Continue, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg, you were talking about how you found out that there might be an Aktion coming from the Germans and that you had better escape with your wife and son. And you were talking about it Mr. Boboski and how he had informed you of the orders from the German high command. Who was the German officer who gave that order to dig the holes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krauzer. Krauzer in that time. Maybe I'm not pronouncing it right. And he was from the capital city, from Dubno. And he came with a car to the office where Boboski was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that officer's name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krauzer. It's a German name. K-R-O-Z-E-R. Krauzer. The first name I don't know. He was an SS officer in the capital city, from Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out what those holes were for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only we're thinking. Not with-- because, when I went to take the information, he didn't know himself. He said they were talking between them. I says, for potatoes, for winter? Because potatoes are used to do [INAUDIBLE]. And some of them say, it's for you people. Some of them. Not exactly. Then I left the house, and I came to home. And I was scared to death. Because the death was waiting. And I came to the house. And my wife used to, you know, make dinner, whatever she does. And I told her the news. And right away, come out. Because my mind from there, I was in the house, changed already what I have to do. I'm not going with the group. I'm going with my group, my own group. My wife and my child. And I took him out, with my wife and I went. And we made in that time, like I told you already, a place in case we succeed to find each other when this war is ending.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you went back into the ghetto that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was an officer, and I couldn't go out. Because I was an officer. And I was already in the ghetto. Like a Jewish tradition, if it's not necessary to tell you-- they took it out. They throw us outside from the synagogue, outside, and prayed to God. People-- which right away, the news was spread in ghetto. And they did. They helped, like, helped-- and they killed them. You see what the help us. They took it out and prayed and cried, but it didn't help. They took them in wagons and brought them to the grave. And this is the picture what I have. What I had the picture, from the grave where they shoot all the Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about escaping that night with your wife and son and going into the woods with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the kid? Of course, this was my plan. I didn't know if I would succeed, because they could shoot me, too. I was the same Jew like any other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was safer for your wife and son to go first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, first.

speaker: interviewer
text: --into the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because not together, because our child, and in case we will succeed on this plan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your wife and son waited for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Waited for me there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They hid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not by the ghetto, somewhere else on the place where we picked. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'd like to talk for one more minute about the killings in the ghetto. We had just been talking about that at the end of the last tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you what I know it. Because I was in the ghetto. But I know what it was. They came with trucks and took them on the trucks and brought them the grave. And there they even didn't take off their clothes. With their clothes. With machine guns and that's where they shoot them. And this is the grave, what I have now.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took people? The Germans took people and put them on trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: On trucks, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And shot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, brought them to the grave. They knew the plan where the grave was digging.

speaker: interviewer
text: And made them stand in the grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course. Did I show you? I have the picture from there. And if there's still time to tell you-- or you don't ask me now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In that time when they killed them-- and after, when I was in the hole, in the foxhole, hiding-- I talked to myself, God. Because the guy came and said some dogs brings out, you know, arms, legs, from the grave. Because it was even not covered enough with dirt. And I said at that time-- and this is in my mind, at that time, it was enough. God, let me live. Because when you are in that bad situation, you talk to God. If you don't believe, so-- you know, being, like, at the bottom, when you talk to God, let me live. I will take care. And when I survived, the first thing I went there and covered up the dirt. More dirt. And that's why in that time I made-- if you ask me this, I made a picture what I have. With a little fence. And not because I'm taking the credit. This was my plan when I was in the grave. In case I will survive, I will do this. And I fulfilled my mind and I made in that time and this is the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a grave in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you in a foxhole?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a foxhole? After I survived. When I went to the woods. And I was wandering in the woods a certain time, I couldn't-- at that time. And then I went-- it came to my mind. Maybe I mentioned the name. The name was-- at night they came sometimes to take for them bread for Sasha and the other names there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sasha and her husband would meet you sometimes in the woods to bring you bread, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find the dog with the bones?

speaker: interviewee
text: The dog? The neighbors. In my-- the guy, this was not a Jew. This was a guy, a Polish guy what I find by him shelter. The name was Shareck, you know. And at night, when I was wandering and looking, because the kid was already frozen, because of winter, stiff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son was getting stiff?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son, yes. Stiff. And at that time, I went to look for somebody. Matter of fact, if it's necessary to tell you the story, when I left and I left my wife in Shabo by Sasha, and I couldn't came-- and I came to the guy-- he came to my mind, because I know. I know the area because I was in business. And I came to Shareck. And he told me-- and I offered him, I will give you something I have. And he knows I was not a poor man. And I will give you this and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, you know what? My sons, they're not home. Of course, he had grown-up sons. Why they weren't there, they will come. They went, like-- for them it was not the same situation for me. The kids, the sons went out there to-- you know, for-- like, for dates, for whatever it is. For them it was not scary.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had him. And I know I came for this reason. I wouldn't go back. Whatever will be, I'm going to wait until-- because he gave me all the idea. He wants to discuss with his sons. And I was there. And the sons came in, and when they came in and they start to talk, it was already too late to go back to my wife where I left her in the store. And that's that, I've got to stay over there. Because in day time I couldn't work, because they would kill me. And it was-- my wife thought I'm not coming back. This means I left her. And she said she will go and report herself with the kid to the killers. But the guy was, like I mentioned you, certain people are nice. I didn't hear she went out to here, maybe in the area, if somebody shoot somebody. And he came back and she-- talked to her. Don't believe it. I know George-- my name is now George, at that time was a different name-- and he wouldn't do this. And I didn't hear nothing. And she [INAUDIBLE]. And the last night I came. And I took my wife with my child-- I'll make it shorter. And I went to them. And I went to the store. When I came back and he told me he already was killed to take me in. He said, oh, we already exchanged. When you left, they mind not to take anyone. To here, to here. Decent people. Don't think everybody was killers. There was decent people, too. And anyway, I went to the store and I brought them in the store, bring it out already for the kid something warm. And the force start to be stronger and cold. And decent people with good feeling, one human to another human. And they came out. And we start to discuss something to do. And we find out, in my mind I had a plan, heard from somebody. They dig in the barn, where the cows. A hole under the cows, and put a wood on the top. And put in there in the cow's back into the kitchen where the cow is eating. Take it off the bottom, and that's where they are. And when I came in, I thought, I'm a man in this house. I wasn't so happy. And so satisfied where I came to the hole, because in the hole under the cows was warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what you've been describing is the first person-- the first family that helped you and your wife and your son in hiding after you escaped the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of this man is Vladek Shareck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shareck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell the name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will try. Vladek, W-L-A-D-E-K, Vladek. And Shareck, C-H-A-R-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Gentile family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's a different subject. When I found the hole, and I was always a feeling for a human. And I left my sister [INAUDIBLE] Ukraine in the beginning. And she was a young girl, 17 years old. And another guy. And my way of thinking was, I know the reason why he left you. Because you are a young girl and he was a young man. And I will try to go and pick you up. I wouldn't mention to my boss that I want to bring it in my sister, because I took permit only for three of us. But I told her, I said, I will do whatever I had to do. And I did. And I went to find out if my sister is still there, because when I left her, it took time. Took a couple moons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: In another village, about 10 kilometers from the place where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of her village?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Nowne, N-O-W-N-E. And the [? button, ?] this, the first, yes. This was the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which sister is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: She survives. She survives with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have pictures. And she just died when she was in Israel. She left-- it's not necessary to know-- four children, three boys and a daughter. Brilliant. Two are in the United States. One is an engineer. Anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually she emigrated to Israel and her sons--

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to Israel--

speaker: interviewer
text: --are here?

speaker: interviewee
text: --after the surviving. And our second cousin came from the army, from the Russians. Didn't find his family. And they both talk it over, a boy and a girl. And they went to Israel. Instead I went to Germany-- they went to Israel from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll talk more about that in a few minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to hear more about Bunya. And you found Bunya hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I found-- yes. And I'll make shorter the story because every detail is-- and I know my son is listening there. He will make a remark. I'm talking now, this is what you asked me. I'm not afraid, but I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bring Bunya with you to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And when I went, that's a nice history for life what I survived. And I will show you now the picture. When I went to pick up Bunya, and in that time there were more believers than now as God did this. And a way-- and I didn't went with the road. I went with holes and you know, in the woods, to pick her up at night. And I see somebody's coming across. And then I thought-- and I was myself. And I thought to myself, it's the end of my life. And I came nearer, I find a name, a Jewish fellow. Matter of fact, he was second cousin to my wife. And the name was Feldman, a famous family, but it's not necessary. I don't want to mention details. It's too long going, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: His last name is Feldman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was, yes. And I found him and we know each other because-- and he told me he was also somewhere in a house with somebody. He didn't mention to me, because everything was a secret at that time if it's happened. Because if somebody, they call the [INAUDIBLE] or the Germans or the SS. Find you and they hate you. And you talk what you know and what you don't know. And for this sake, I didn't ask him where. But he told me he's got also a child and he gave it away to somebody. And I didn't ask him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He gave his child away to somebody?

speaker: interviewee
text: To somebody, that's all. And then we shake hands or we didn't shake hands. We said goodbye to each other, and I went. And then I went to my sister and I picked up her. I find my sister and I picked her up and went back. And I came back to the hole. And in three or four weeks, in a certain time, my guy came and told me, you know, they killed today Gus Feldman.

speaker: interviewer
text: They killed him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. With his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And she was-- they both were hiding by a Ukrainian woman in the house. And then it was about three, four weeks, five weeks, I don't know exactly the time. And then she was scared and this and this. And she went to report themselves, that she had the Jews. And they took them and they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainian woman who was hiding them in her house, she got scared and reported that they were hiding? And what was the first name of Mr. Feldman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, Feldman's name? Yossel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the first name of his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife? One second. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] A famous name, not a Jewish name. Because they were both-- I mean, I'm mixing up. If you don't ask me on this question. But I came to this subject, and I can mention. The finest people in that time, in our generation, in that time and this. And she was-- I have to-- the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: It may come to you later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it will came.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it does, just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nella.

speaker: interviewer
text: --let me know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nella. N-E-- not a Jewish name. But fine, educated people. And a little bit further relations with them, with my wife's relation. Anyway, that's not the main thing. And when I heard, I mentioned when I was in the-- the guy came and said, you know, they killed today Yossel Feldman. That's why the name came. Because I remembered when he came.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were-- go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you ask me this, what I came, it's a good subject. And this-- and this discussion. And I said, oy, vey, God. Let me live, God. Let me live, and they killed him. Because he told me in that time, and the son, he gave it away. If I will survive, I will look for the kid. And this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you promised that you would look for their child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I promised to God in that time. And when I survived I always had a mind, I will do it. And I'm telling you because I wanted the story, because this is life history. And a human decent nation, what can take in this subject, on this area in that time. In my time when it was. And when I survived, I didn't forget. And I start to look for the kid. I'll make shorter the story. And in that time, meantime, I heard a Polack, there's a kid, a girl. And I heard was in my mind. Up there is Aviva. Because Aviva was the Jewish name, not a Hebrew name, that the parents gave to the girl in that time. I have the picture in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Aviva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I will spell you. We'll come to this. If you need it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewee
text: A-V-A-V-- the way you say it in Jewish, you know. Yeah, I will spell the name somehow. And when I survived, I went to there because I had in mind, I heard that this Polack is a little kid, a girl. And when I survived, the first thing I went to see-- and I didn't see. and this is the kid. Because the kid I know, because it was a little-- And I start to look, you know. And in that-- in that time, on this-- and it's happened, a Jewish lady came to this little city where I survived. And I was living-- not in my city, in Mylnov, in a house. And a lady came in looking for her kid. Because she left-- she was a Jewish lady and left her kid. It's a story, it's connected to this story. And that's why I'm trying to go away from this what-- And she left a kid there and there. And it came to my mind, in that time in [INAUDIBLE]. The guy came from the church, my [INAUDIBLE] because he was Polish. A Polish guy brought a kid to the church and said he find ways where they shot a kid with a little note that this is not Jewish. It's a Polish, was Christian. In Czestochowa. I'll tell you exactly what's connected on to the history, what I became. And in that time-- and some lady took the kid. In that time it entered my mind that this is the kid which Feldman left. Anyway, it came to the mind. That's why I mixed up the story. I couldn't control myself, because it came to my mind, the [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, I find out, this is not a girl. And then I heard another history, that a guy-- you know, the guy, the Polish guy, brought her to the church. And the nuns, he find a note-- I didn't finish the story-- that the kid, she's not Jewish. Because it was in that time, the same time what the Jews was wondering, from the ghettos, they'll have killed her. That it's not a Jewish kid, the name is so-and-so. It was Christian. In Czestochowa. I still remember the thing. And a guy took him and I thought, in that time, that this is the kid. I know they will make a remark because I mixed up already certain things, because they know the history. Anyway, it's not this kid. And a certain time later-- I came to the subject-- a lady came to my house. And when I went to look for the kid and I saw that it's not Aviva the same-- but I went to her. And then a couple months later, I don't remember the time exactly, a lady came in, a Jewish lady, to me. And I used to live in the house. And I don't want to, because we'll drag it too long, certain details which came to my mind when I talk to you, which is not necessary in the history. And I came to-- and a lady came into my house and told me-- after we were liberated. She is a Jewish lady. And she left her kid somewhere. And I looked in here. And she looked exactly like the kid what I went in the beginning to the lady what she-- the kid, she never got-- And I told her, this is not a make-up story. This is my story from my life. I said, I know where the kid is. And I took the lady, the mother, and I went to the lady where she picked up the kid. Exactly like if God created the features from both of them and this is their kid. And I'll make shorter the story. We took out the kid and gave it to the mother. With help-- I mixed up shorter and longer the story, because I went too deep to tell every detail, to explain every detail. But it's connected.

speaker: interviewer
text: So  you're telling us that the Feldmans' daughter, Aviva Feldman--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Aviva I find later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. You found her later? And she had been taken care of by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a Christian family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Aviva-- at night when I find-- this came to my mind and you just asked me. Because I already mixed up. I know they said-- my Ger-- my son sitting there, because I mixed up already. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --this is my mind. My mind is not the same. And the history is also connected one to another one. But I mixed up, not in the right time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe what we'll do at this point is continue talking about the people who helped you and your wife to stay in hiding. And then we will, in a little while, pick up the story again of Aviva Feldman and how you found her.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, good. Thank you for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now when you remind me, yes. It is a history--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So let's go back to the people who helped you, the Gentile families who helped you. You were talking about the first farmer you stayed with was Vladek Shareck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shareck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and your wife and your son, Gerry, stayed--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister, Bunya, stayed under the floor of the barn. And you were there for how long about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And then why did you leave that barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Ukrainian people and the Polish people started to fight each other. And they burned up the farm. And the [? barn ?] too. And I will make shorter the story, because I went too deep already with the other story  to this. It would take more than a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Ukrainian people were fighting, and what did they do to the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Burned up. And I was burned. And I took my wife. I said, take a child. And I took my Gerry at that time, and [? I also ?] went out to the field at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you put your baby out in the field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, baby there. And I left my wife with my sister together there. Because my wife at that time was not feeling-- because she was under the dirt-- she couldn't, her back was hurt from the-- the dampness from the grave. Because we was in a grave, in a hole, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her legs were hurt from the dampness in the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I left her with my sister, and I took the child and I went. In that time it was summer time already and on the field the corn or whatever it is was in there. And then my sister went with my wife. Somehow she took her out from the scare, you know, and they went out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Somehow your wife managed to get out with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow, yeah. With my sister. And near to the morning when it start to-- we find each other. I make shorter already the story, because it's a longer story. And I find myself and then. And we were in the day time I left them. When I find them, we were lucky to find each other. With scare and also not with life. 90% still waiting to be shot or killed. But survived. And I went to look for another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that other place?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Another thing was another village. And then I went-- I left them in the field because the wheat, or the corn, was high. And they were laying there. And I left them there. And I said, I will be back. In-- I didn't know when. And I went in another village, because I know the area. And there-- so it's another history. But maybe it's too much to tell you. It came to my mind. God gave me always the mind to help myself in that situation which I was. I will go to this, the name was-- Ivan Lishvwicz. His son was a policeman by the Germans when the Germans came in. And the Germans start to [INAUDIBLE] already to people, where they came from radio, the Germans start to lose the war in Stalingrad. Stalingrad, I don't know if you're familiar with this. And they're going back. And it came to my mind, I will try to do this. And I came to Ivan. And I find him-- I make shorter also this story, because it's too deep. And I know he'll have a remark, because I got away from the history. But this is connected to the history what I tell you, how I survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came in, I find him, Ivan. And I start to explain. He was scared when he saw me, thought I will kill him. Because at that time it was already a year or more that Jews was killed. And they were afraid. When you're in the woods, you come be a killer, too. You know? That's what's on their minds. And find me, and anyway, and I ask him-- I make shorter. And I told him, Ivan, you know what's waiting for you. You saw us, and the Germans going back. They already start to lose the war. You know what's waiting for you. And I have-- we were five brothers. Not sleepy brothers, you know? And I didn't know my-- I will finish. I make--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother is alive-- I didn't know he was alive. But I told him, in case he's got something in my mind different, he should be afraid for my brothers. I tell you because it's connected to the history from my life, how I survived. And I used at that time my common sense what I had in that time. And-- and I said, Ivan, you know what's waiting for you. Your son was a policeman. Still, there's Germans here. You know what's waiting for you. Plain talk, [INAUDIBLE]. And my brothers, I mentioned the name because he knew them, of course, in the life. And take me-- and he has a good nature, too. Because otherwise he would take-- and he risked his life. He took me in. And I was for him eight months on the same make-- and I was by him eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he hid you in his barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In his barn, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: For eight months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then the Germans came to take away from the village, from where I was, I was in another village. From the farmers a certain thing. And the lady was scared. I made sure nobody was scared. And afterward the lady told me, I should go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you, Mister--

speaker: interviewee
text: She risked her life, and this is the end of [INAUDIBLE]. I came to another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, thank you, Mr. Steinberg. We'll pick up--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for--

speaker: interviewer
text: --from this in just--
